data.title: SentinelOne: The AI Risk Is Overblown
data.description:
Cybersecurity stocks might not be fully safe from the AI threat after all. SentinelOne (S) has traded with some weakness, though notably not to the same extent as many software peers amidst the AI reckoning. The company is still posting strong growth rates, but profitability is a concern, as well as potential pressures from AI. The valuation looks quite buyable, although the relative discount has been addressed by unusual means. I reiterate my buy rating for the stock.

SentinelOne Stock Price

I last covered SentinelOne in January, where I rated the stock a buy on account of the valuation. The stock is down around 16% since.

Compared to any software peers, the stock is not down “that much,” but I note that this is likely due to the cybersecurity focus as well as the stock’s low valuation heading into the AI volatility.

SentinelOne Stock Key Metrics

SentinelOne is a cybersecurity endpoint protection company. This means that the company helps protect endpoints such as laptops and phones) while offering other cybersecurity products in addition to that.

Historically, the endpoint protection market has been a highly fragmented space. This has benefitted leading innovators like CrowdStrike (CRWD) and SentinelOne, as they have been able to show accelerated growth not only from a growing market but also by taking market share. In recent years, however, CRWD’s wider portfolio appears to have given it a leg up as customers have shown increasing preference for vendor consolidation.

Even so, SentinelOne continues to show solid growth, with revenue growing 20% YoY in the quarter to $271 million, meeting guidance.

The company made some progress on profitability, with non-GAAP operating margins jumping over 400 bps to 5.7%, exceeding guidance of 5%.

I, however, must acknowledge the slim profit margin as well as note that the company has not made meaningful progress on reducing the GAAP operating loss. This is in stark contrast with CRWD and other cybersecurity peers, which often boast generous profit margins.

The company boasts strong 96% gross retention rates. I expect investors to remain razor-focused on GRR as a way to monitor potential disruption from AI.

SentinelOne ended the quarter with $770 million of cash versus no debt. That strong net cash balance sheet offsets any concerns regarding profitability, at least from a financial solvency perspective.

Looking ahead, management guided the company to see around $278 million in revenue in the first quarter, representing an acceleration to 21.4% YoY growth. The full-year guide for $1.205 billion represents 20.4% growth.

On the conference call, management noted that “non-endpoint solutions surpassed half of our total annual bookings.” That is an important data point, as the company has been diversifying away from their core endpoint protection products in past years. Management believes that AI is a “long-term tailwind for our business,” as they are positioned to “emerge as the category winner in the AI era.” While I am bullish on the stock, I believe some skepticism may be warranted given CRWD’s strong results. Management noted that their “customer base is still relatively underpenetrated,” underscoring the continued opportunity in consolidating the endpoint market.

Is SentinelOne Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

SentinelOne, along with other cybersecurity stocks, has traded with extreme volatility ever since rumors surfaced regarding Anthropic’s secret model being a potential cybersecurity competitor. That volatility accelerated when the model was everything the rumor promised and more. As of recent prices, SentinelOne traded at just around 3.4x this year’s sales.

Consensus estimates call for the company to sustain healthy double-digit growth for a long time. It is worth noting that, unlike in the past many years, SentinelOne actually is not trading at a big discount to many software names. Sure, it still trades at a large discount to cybersecurity peers, but on a price-to-sales basis, the stock trades more or less in line with names like Salesforce (CRM) in spite of lacking the same profitability. At this point, the company’s stronger growth is offset by the poor profit margins. This is in contrast to the past, when the stock traded at a large discount to names like CRM, when the latter’s higher profit margins more than offset the slower growth rate. This is not the way that I had hoped for the discount to be addressed, and this is why I hesitate on upgrading the stock. Because SentinelOne primarily prices on a per-endpoint basis, this does inherently give it exposure to potential pressures to seats as AI might lead to widespread job displacements. This is a risk that has only been magnified by Claude Mythos and the continual releases of new AI models.

That said, not only is cybersecurity unlikely to be top of mind in terms of budget scrutiny, but I would expect the tailwinds behind endpoint protection to still remain strong in spite of any such pressures, somewhat similar to how leading e-commerce names performed strongly in spite of past negative cycles. It is admittedly concerning that the company is still this far away from GAAP profitability, but I continue to expect operating leverage to be in the company’s future. I typically expect these software companies to achieve net margins of at least 30% over the long term. Many cybersecurity peers are enjoying robust profit margins already. Due to SentinelOne’s current gap from profitability, I instead assume around 20% net margins over the long term. That would place the stock at around 20x long-term earnings, which looks like a reasonable multiple given the high top-line growth. But that growth is offset by both the company’s lack of profitability as well as the potential competitive pressures from CRWD and any future AI competitors. The ongoing GAAP losses are offset by the large net cash balance sheet, but all in all I would say that there are clear reasons why the valuation should not be considered dirt cheap.

SentinelOne Stock Risks

It is possible that generative AI elevates the competition. It is possible that AI leads to requiring a new set of cybersecurity tools. It is possible that AI leads to cybersecurity breaches. It is possible that management is unable to drive margins higher. The company is not yet profitable on a GAAP basis and may experience considerable volatility in an economic downturn.

SentinelOne Stock Conclusion

SentinelOne is still offering aggressive growth rates for those searching for growth in software. I expect endpoint cybersecurity to remain an important secular theme even in the age of AI. I am hopeful that management makes a more committed effort towards driving margins higher, as that appears to be a clear reason behind the stock’s large relative discount to cybersecurity peers. SentinelOne is the kind of stock I look for in the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I reiterate my buy rating for the stock.

data.title: PVH: Missed Targets Lead To Clear Short Opportunity
data.description:
PVH Corp. (PVH) is the classic value trap of the consumer sector. It's very cheap on all sorts of traditional valuation metrics and has a very positive, outward management team but has, over time, lacked the consistency of an earnings compounder that other companies within the consumer sector have had. As such, it's become a high-beta vehicle for traders and investors alike to express clear views when there are rapid shifts in the consumer landscape. Recently, the stock has lifted its head quite quickly post-fiscal Q4 earnings, but this is not the time to buy, and the stock is actually expensive if properly valued.

How Did We Get Here?

PVH is not a complicated story—it's the owner of Calvin Klein and Tommy Hilfiger, two highly global brands with storied histories. The problem with these brands is not that they aren't recognizable or aren't of rich heritage; it's that they've become overdistributed and, therefore, undesirable. As a result, management in the past few years has been pushing the PVH+ Plan—a plan designed to elevate the brand, lead with a few key “hero” products, clean up the portfolio in terms of distribution touchpoints, and, finally, start to expand margins.

The plan was to produce an HSD% compound annual growth rate for revenue to a 2025 target of $12.5 billion, produce an operating margin target of 15% and free cash flow of $1 billion. The company at the time also authorized a $1bn stock repurchase program.

Fast forward, and the fiscal year for 2025 is now complete—revenues are just about $9 billion, gross margins are down 60bps vs. 2022, and adjusted operating margins are roughly 10%. This is a huge miss versus their plan. Even adjusted for the Heritage sale two years ago (a business they divested that was non-core), you're looking at perhaps the most egregious undershooting vs. an investor day plan that exists in the consumer space.

Stock Setup

What caught my eye recently was a clear break above the 200-day moving average, and the stock is just now sitting on the trend line for its long-term downtrend that started in Q1 2024. The stock has been rallying since it reported earnings:

Was the move higher warranted? The company's Q4 was “fine” by Wall Street's low estimates, but you have to remember, a company like PVH tends to beat their forward quarter estimates. In fact, history shows with this company that they have a tendency to guide the forward quarter on a non-GAAP EPS basis very conservatively so they can deliver consistent earnings beats, all the while the needle never actually moves on profitability over time.

So, I do not believe the move higher post-earnings is warranted. This is a heavily shorted stock in the consumer space and has been a massive underperformer vs. better global brands like Ralph Lauren (RL) and Tapestry (TPR). The positioning of management in this latest earnings was to offer up guidance where they called for:

  • Slight revenue increase
  • Operating margins “stable” at 8.8% on a non-GAAP basis

Essentially, management is telling you there's nothing to get excited about in the year ahead. With an earnings compounder, you would call this conservative and look for reasons as to why this is conservative. The issue is that PVH has little history of executing upon their stated goals. This is essentially a business with worst-in-class margins, revenue growth in the last few years that is off-algorithm, and no clear strategy to grow. There are several issues here.

First, PVH is too big and needs to shrink to grow. This is very similar to the problems that Under Armour (UAA) faced in the 2010s. PVH has a $9 billion revenue footprint—that's very large relative to brands like Ralph Lauren, similarly well recognized with a rich heritage, that do $7.8 billion in sales but at nearly double the operating margin. It's not that a company in consumer can't do $9 billion in sales and run a double-digit operating profile; it's that their exposures and strategy do not mesh with what the brand is offering.

If we take a step back and think about what's led to RL being a great stock over the past 5 years, it's really been driven by methodical growth in DTC and pricing power. Wholesale was de-emphasized, especially as the N. American department store channel was losing foot traffic, negatively impacting sales. PVH is overexposed to the wholesale channel globally and underpenetrated to the US consumer, who boasts the largest spending power. Just 30% of the business, approximately, is in N. America. This is in contrast to other global brands that tend to have at least 50% exposure to N. America (usually, of this N. American mix, Canada/Mexico account for LSD% contribution).

So, for the investor at home looking to play a rebound in consumer stocks after a tough start to the year for the sector (XRT is down 5% YTD) on the back of higher gasoline prices, geopolitical tensions, and weak spending coupled with a colder winter that dampened shopping occasions, is the smart move really to go and buy a stock like PVH with no defined strategy for success?

To be fair, tariffs have been a rather large headwind to PVH. After having overcome the challenges of severe input cost inflation immediately post-COVID, with the company on track to finally get above 60% adjusted gross margins and perhaps 11-12% operating margins on a sustained basis, tariffs came into play last year. While the company had an advantage vs. more domestic peers in terms of revenue concentration, the margins are so slim for PVH, and without pricing power, the tariffs cannot be offset. Tariffs were a 215bp headwind to operating margins in 2025, and while fiscal Q1 will show a commensurate hit, it's in the base now. Unless tariffs get rolled back, this company is structurally less profitable than it was a few years ago.

But, past-tariffs, which are a below-the-revenue-line phenomenon, how do we make sense of the revenue shortfall for PVH vs. their HSD% growth target? The brand elevation that was set forth in the 2022 Investor Day has simply not happened. There were periods where it did look like it could happen (2023/2024), but they were rather short-lived. PVH has not grown revenue in its Investor Day period (2022-2025)—in fact, revenues are down a couple of points since those targets were given. On an ex-Heritage basis, revenue is essentially flat. Keep in mind that's in contrast to peers RL and TPR, who have grown revenues at an MSD% CAGR.

The market got this right on the way down, too. Look at the performance divergence in the last 3 years, which corresponds with the company's multi-year plan window. Long PVH/short RL would've been a widow-maker. PVH is down 7% on a 3Y basis, while RL is up 206.7%. That's simply incredible. Below is the relative spread between the two securities. This further enforces that rips in the stock of PVH should be faded.

Value Trap

Earnings are the wrong way to value PVH. The company tends to have significant below-the-line items, so adj. EBITDA is the preferred way to actually model it out.

What I find to be most interesting is that with the recent move in the stock, the shares are nearly approaching a one standard deviation high on EV/EBITDA. I think the pivotal fact about this company is that it has not grown operating profit (2025: $791 million) since the early 2010s. You have to go back nearly 15 years to have operating profit dollar growth. That's with revenue growth of nearly 60% since the early 2010s. This company spends money, and it never shows up in the hands of shareholders.

So, when should investors get involved? 15-20% lower from the long side. From here, the stock screens more as a short. There seems to be very little happening, and at an unattractive valuation level, there's not much to do. I find it interesting that the company has not yet signaled for a new Investor Day, despite their '22 targets having now expired. Even on an ex-tariff basis, this company missed their own operating margin by several hundred basis points.

Conclusion

Until the company shrinks to grow, in that it de-emphasizes global wholesale channels and actually shows signs of being able to elevate their flagship brands like peers have, there is little reason to chase runs in the stock. The stock may screen cheap, but relative to its own history, this is not the place to run to as a consumer trying to play a rebound into the second half of the year.

data.title: Gilat Satellite Networks: Better Business, But Less Upside Than Before
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Gilat Satellite Networks (GILT) is no longer the obvious deep-value rerating opportunity it was at $11.65, but I still see enough execution and momentum to justify further upside. What changed, beyond the stock price, is that the business now has clearer momentum in in-flight connectivity, growing defense exposure, and a more favorable operating environment for satellite infrastructure. The fourth quarter of 2025 (which I will discuss in greater detail in this article) confirmed the presence of growth in the indicated areas even if Stellar Blu continues to put pressure on margins. This is therefore an execution story and not simply a "the valuation makes no sense" story.

What Changed Since My Last Article

A few months ago, I wrote an article about Gilat Satellite Networks in which I was strongly positive on the stock (in fact, the rating was "strong buy"), with a share price of $11.65. At the time of writing, the stock is around $17.71, so I can already say the market has proven me right. Beyond that, in recent months, something else interesting has also moved, beyond the price. For example, Gilat has disclosed over $75 million in contracted business in 2026 alone, across three distinct markets including aviation, defense and commercial satellite communications. Not bad for a company with a market capitalization of $1.3 billion!

Of greatest interest to me is the increased pace of development within the in-flight connectivity market. In addition to securing two large orders totaling $39 million for Sidewinder terminals [ESA], Gilat recently entered into several additional agreements with both avionics manufacturers and system integrators [IFC]. The combination of these events suggests that the product is transitioning toward a more commercially viable and less technically focused stage. One of the major areas of focus regarding Gilat’s aerospace offering was whether it would evolve beyond being a conference call theme and become a meaningful contributor to overall revenues. It appears that the answer may now be affirmative.

Additionally, the defense side of the business has improved as well. The recent award of over $16 million for DKET 3421 transportable terminals to a European Ministry of Defense represents more than just the dollar amount. It represents Gilat’s first sale to that ministry and provides access to other potential customers through European sovereign programs. Such programs typically move slowly to adoption, but tend to provide greater stability once they do adopt. Additionally, the $9 million contract signed with the Israeli Ministry of Defense combined with the advancements made in virtualizing architectures [SATCOM] enhance Gilat’s credibility as a provider of technologies rather than merely hardware.

The second area of change has received less attention than the first, however, it may be equally as impactful. What I mean is that Gilat’s operating environment is significantly better than it was when I wrote my last article. The demonstration of 5G connectivity on satellite [GEO] compliant with 3GPP standards demonstrates that Gilat is not simply profiting from the current cycle, but rather positioning itself for inclusion in future generations of satellite based infrastructure. Furthermore, the regulatory environment is improving as evidenced by the loosening of power limits by the FCC to enable higher capacity satellite broadband service delivery and the establishment of a clearer regulatory framework in India. While neither is sufficient to support a thesis in isolation, collectively they represent a positive tailwind for a relatively small-cap such as Gilat.

Q4 2025 Earnings Overview

Gilat’s Q4 2025 financials are difficult to question. For instance, total revenue for the quarter was $137.0 million which represented a 75 percent increase over the prior year and a 16 percent quarterly sequential increase.

Additionally, the total amount of revenue realized in 2025 was $451.7 million representing a 48 percent increase over the same period in 2024. Non-GAAP net earnings per share (EPS) for the quarter totaled $0.20 exceeding the analysts’ estimate of $0.14.

Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter totaled $18.2 million, an improvement of 50 percent over the prior year. However, it is where the growth occurred in each respective segment that makes the report interesting. Total commercial segment revenue increased to $75.1 million during the quarter or 103 percent when comparing to the same period last year.

Much of that increase is due to In-Flight Connectivity and the acquisition of Stellar Blu. Defense segment revenue increased to $33.3 million; a 14 percent increase over the prior year. The Peru segment posted record quarterly revenue of $28.5 million, up 141% year over year.

That is, there is no single area contributing to Gilat’s growth; rather, the Company demonstrated increasing momentum in each of its core segments.

That being said, Gilat’s quarter was “not perfect” and I believe that is where the market paused. The primary issue remaining for Gilat is Stellar Blu. Management indicated that, despite recent additions to its manufacturing capacity, the production ramp continues to be margin compressive. As such, the GAAP Gross Margin declined from 40 percent last year to 28 percent in this year’s fourth quarter. Similarly, Gilat’s cash flow quality was disappointing during the fourth quarter. Operating cash flow was negative during the fourth quarter totaling -$6.3 million and Days Sales Outstanding (DSO) increased to 88 days from 68 days at September 30th. Although these two items did not diminish the strength of the fourth quarter for Gilat, they did remind us that Gilat is still in a transitional stage of growth. The efficiency story is still ahead of us and not fully visible yet.

What really caught my eye about this release was the underlying implications within the disclosure itself. During the fourth quarter of 2025 Gilat delivered approximately 190 In-flight connectivity terminals. Further, Gilat ended 2025 with a significantly improved balance sheet as a result of capital raised during the year.

Finally, although Gilat offered limited detail regarding specific forecasts related to their revenues and profitability for calendar year 2026 ($500 million – $520 million in revenue; $61 million – $66 million in adjusted EBITDA), Gilat does provide some insight into how we should view those projections.

Specifically, Gilat has provided guidance for each of its reporting segments (Commercial, Defense, Peru). While those estimates represent modest increases over what we saw in 2025, they suggest that we are likely looking at another year of steady growth and not an isolated peak. The forecasted amounts include

  • Commercial at $315 million – $335 million;
  • Defense at $115 million – $130 million;
  • and Peru at $60 million – $65 million

which is closer to historical norms following last year’s abnormally high level of construction activity in that country.

Therefore, yes, Q4 was strong, but more importantly, the real takeaway is that Gilat appears to be showing signs that 2025 was not just a fluke.

Valuation: No Longer Cheap, Still Not Crazy

Gilat’s valuation is somewhat harder to discern. On a trailing basis, the stock trades at about 43.3x P/E, 22.7x EV/EBITDA, and 2.9x Price/Sales (source: Bloomberg Terminal). As you’d expect, if someone just looks at the P/E ratio, the stock looks expensive. And honestly, compared strictly with comparable companies, it is expensive. Seeking Alpha’s valuation grade of C- confirms this.

However, I don’t believe P/E ratio is the most effective method to assess Gilat currently. The Earnings Per Share (EPS) growth curve will continue to be affected by the increased revenue from the Stellar Blu ramp-up, lower margins due to rising costs, and a transitional year in which the rate of revenue growth exceeds normal rates of normalized EPS. That is why the forecasted numbers appear so irregular. The Street is essentially pricing a more subdued 2026 EPS profile prior to a significant recovery in 2027. Since forecasts have moved this quickly, P/E ratios reflect more how soon the analyst believes Gilat will reach its potential rather than how good of a company Gilat is.

Compared to growth and sales, there is a much clearer view. Gilat grew revenues by approximately 47.9% YOY. Compared to peer companies including GlobalStar, Iridium Communications and Viasat, which grow their revenues by somewhere between 3% to 9% annually, Gilat's 47.9% annual growth rate is significantly higher.

However, when evaluating price-to-sales multiples, Gilat is trading at 2.5x Sales. For example, Iridium Communications trades at a much higher multiple of 6x sales; however, Gilat is significantly lower than GlobalStar's 34x Sales Multiple and is higher than Viasat's 2.85x Sales Multiple. Therefore, yes, Gilat's stock is priced as a premium for growth, but, it is not priced at a fantasy level.

The larger debate centers on EV/EBITDA. At 26.7x, Gilat is clearly higher than Iridium at 11x and Viasat at 10.1x. Clearly, the premium paid is real and investors need to accept it rather than deny it. However, I feel that it is at least partially warranted. The revenue growth rate for Gilat is increasing significantly faster than all of its competitors. Additionally, bookings momentum for Gilat has improved, and management continues to guide for both top line and EBITDA growth in 2026. If Stellar Blu margins meet expectations, then today's EBITDA multiple may ultimately appear less severe than what appears on a static screen.

Target Price

Street analysts estimate similar results. Street consensus estimates are around $19.00. Analysts from Needham estimate $20.00, and Freedom Broker estimated $18.00.

In my opinion, this is less of a valuation doesn't make any sense story line. Instead, we now are dealing with an execution issue. If management can meet their 2026 forecast of $500 million to $520 million in revenue and $61 million to $66 million in adjusted EBITDA, and demonstrate continued reductions in Stellar Blu's margin impacts; then I believe a fair value range for the shares would be approximately $19.00 to $23.00 per share. A conservative base case of $22.00 to $23.00 per share will be defendable; however a bull case of $26.00 could potentially exist if several positive factors occurred simultaneously:

  • margins normalize quickly;
  • defense conversion rates increase;
  • and large In-Flight Connectivity (IFC) win(s).

Three Key Risks for Gilat Satellite Networks

The bull case for Gilat makes sense now. What is difficult is to admit the ways that things can still go wrong. There are three risk factors that I believe are far more significant.

The First Thing I Would Watch Is Whether Stellar Blu Will Eventually Be Able To Scale Profitably

Q4 confirmed that Stellar Blu is helping Gilat's top line. However, it confirmed that it is still hurting its margins. Gilat's Gross Margin dropped significantly in Q4. The Company indicated that the main reason for the decline in Gross Margin was due to both the Production Ramp-Up and Amortization of Acquired Intangible Assets. This is important since one of the key elements of the valuation case for Gilat is that Stellar Blu will stop being an expense and begin to look similar to the rest of the business. If Stellar Blu becomes profitable, then the 2026 EBITDA Guidance appears achievable and possibly overly conservative. If Stellar Blu does not become profitable, then Gilat will continue to be viewed by the markets as a revenue story with unpredictable profitability. As such, typically, this limits the potential upside.

The Second Issue That Is Less Visible, But Not Ignorable, Is Working Capital

Another area of concern for me is less obvious. Q4 cash flow from operations was negative. DSO increased to 88 days in Q4 from 68 in Q3. When a company grows as quickly as Gilat is growing, working capital issues are somewhat expected. Even though revenue grew rapidly during Q4 and Cash Conversion did not improve at the same rate; I focus on this type of performance. It does not necessarily indicate an issue with Gilat, but it does indicate that part of the growth in revenue is consuming cash and not flowing down to net income as cleanly as possible. With the capital raise completed in 2025 and the resulting increase in Gilat's balance sheet strength; Gilat is not experiencing a liquidity crisis. The issue here is more nuanced: if cash continues to be consumed by working capital; Gilat's Management Team may have fewer options for making additional acquisition-related investments. Additionally, they may have less room for aggressively pursuing new opportunities, which ultimately could limit their ability to achieve a higher Price Per Share.

Timing Of Defense Segment Growth Is Always A Challenge

I understand why investors prefer to focus on the Defense segment. Typically, it carries greater strategic value, customer retention/stickiness, and better margins. Where there is always a challenge in terms of government-linked revenue is converting those revenues into cash on an investor's preferred schedule. Gilat already had some experience with administrative delays related to recognizing defense revenue. While bookings were strong, Commercial Revenue outperformed defense revenue (reported) growth in Q4. I do not think that should be overlooked. While defense can certainly provide a long-term source of growth for Gilat; it can create challenges on a quarter-to-quarter basis. Therefore, if the acceleration of Defense referenced in the 2026 guidance is delayed due to procurement scheduling issues, budget related friction, or simply bureaucratic delays; the profit implications could be even worse than a pure revenue miss.

Conclusion

Given all this information the "easy money" has probably already been made. I continue to believe there is potential for Gilat to grow and therefore have an upside from where we are today. However, I don't currently feel the "asymmetry" exists at levels I required when considering a "Strong Buy" designation several months ago. While Gilat is not inexpensive at present, Gilat must demonstrate Stellar Blu can successfully scale while maintaining greater profit margins. In addition, we will need to pay close attention to how well Gilat manages working capital. This doesn't necessarily invalidate my thesis, however, it changes the way we consider the opportunity.

Gilat is a "Buy", however, it is not a "Strong Buy". I still believe the price per share for Gilat can move towards the $19 to $23 fair value range. Any additional upside will depend on clean execution in margin expansion, conversion of defense contracts into cash flows, and additional In-Flight Connectivity (IFC) wins. Existing shareholders should continue to maintain their position within Gilat. New investors should be interested in purchasing shares of Gilat. However, they should be somewhat selective and significantly less aggressive than those who purchased shares during November.

data.title: Pentair: One Of The Best Ways To Play The Secular Growth Theme In Water
data.description:
Pentair plc (PNR) is an American global water treatment company. Founded in 1966, Pentair is now a $14 billion (by market cap) water treatment solutions leader employing 9,000 people. The company reports results across three segments: Pool, 38% of FY 2025 sales; Flow, 37%; and Water Solutions, 25%. Pentair manufactures a range of water treatment solution products for a variety of end uses, including filtration systems, pressure tanks, pumps, and spray nozzles.

As I’ve stated before, I think clean, usable, accessible water will be the “liquid gold” of the coming century, just as oil has been for the last century. Water is our most precious resource. While we cannot viably have a modern-day society without petroleum, we literally cannot survive without water. This level of necessity puts companies that facilitate the accessibility and usability of clean water in an extremely favorable position over the coming years.

Pentair is one such company. Moreover, being a global leader in this area, Pentair stands to disproportionately benefit from these secular growth dynamics. And that’s what puts it in a fantastic spot as it relates to being able to continue growing its revenue, profit, and dividend.

Dividend Growth, Growth Rate, Payout Ratio and Yield

Already, Pentair is an absolute legend when it comes to reliable dividend growth. The company has increased its dividend for 50 consecutive years – a newly crowned Dividend King. However, because of a massive spin-off of its electric business via nVent Electric plc (NVT) in 2018, its dividend growth rate over the last decade has fluctuated quite a bit. Zooming on more recent dividend growth, which reflects the pure-play Pentair that exists now, we can see a three-year dividend growth rate of 6%.

And the last two dividend raises came in at 8% or higher. I think this high-single-digit dividend growth of late gives us a good read on what Pentair is capable of and likely will deliver on a go-forward basis. With a payout ratio of only 27.3%, there’s certainly plenty of headroom to move the dividend meaningfully higher. However, the trade-off one has to make in this case, which is common across the entire water theme broadly, is current yield.

Because of the appeal of secular growth within this space, a lot of stocks with a strong attachment to almost anything related to water tend to have high market demand, pushing yields down. To that point, this stock’s yield is only 1.3%. This yield is in line with its own five-year average, meaning this is what the market has been willing to accept from this stock. Those who are more in need of current income (such as retirees) will find this yield problematic. But if one has a long time horizon and can allow the compounding process to play out, they’ll likely look at the long-term potential and like what they see.

Revenue and Earnings Growth

As likable as all of that may be, though, the dividend metrics are mainly based on what’s already happened. However, investors must always be thinking what’s yet to occur, as the capital of today is risked for the rewards of tomorrow. Thus, I’ll now build out a forward-looking growth trajectory for the business, which will be of aid during the valuation process.

I’ll first show you what the business has done over the last seven years in terms of its top-line and bottom-line growth. And I’ll then reveal a professional prognostication for near-term profit growth. Lining up the proven past with a future forecast in this way should give us a baseline understanding of where the business could be going from here. While I usually show a decade’s worth of growth, I’m only using seven years in this case because that aforementioned 2018 spin-off of nVent had a major impact on Pentair’s financials.

Pentair grew its revenue from $3 billion in FY 2019 to $4.2 billion in FY 2025. That’s a compound annual growth rate of 5.8%. That’s a solid rate of top-line growth from a large, mature company that spun out a very dynamic portion of its business. Notably, nVent Electric’s market cap of $19 billion actually exceeds that of Pentair by a healthy margin. Just imagine what a still-combined company could have done!

Meanwhile, earnings per share increased from $2.09 to $3.96 over this period, which is a CAGR of 11.2%. For Pentair to do this after losing a world-class operation is awfully impressive and showcases the strength of the business model and the power of secular growth within water-related solutions.

Looking forward, CFRA is projecting a 9% compound annual growth rate for Pentair’s EPS over the next three years. Rising CapEx across municipalities relating to strict new standards around PFAS is a huge catalyst for Pentair. Water utilities left and right are spending big on water treatment, and Pentair is an obvious beneficiary of this. And that’s on top of the healthy filtration demand from residential and commercial customers.

Plus, there’s the large pool business that takes advantage of a huge installed base with captive customers whereby sensitive and proprietary components need intermittent replacement (and no rational customer is going to risk a very expensive pool in order to save a few bucks on maintenance).

This EPS growth forecast looks quite reasonable to me, and it builds in a scenario where low-double-digit dividend growth is affordable and realistic over the near term (by virtue of the low payout ratio). Pairing that with the starting yield gets one to a low-teens type of annualized total return setup from here, assuming no change in multiples. Getting that on a Dividend King with incredible revenue visibility and secular growth is obviously very appealing.

Financial Position

Moving over to the balance sheet, Pentair has a very good financial position. The long-term debt/equity ratio is 0.4, while the interest coverage ratio is 12. The long-term debt load of about $1.6 billion is not egregious or cumbersome for a company of this size. Pentair has investment-grade credit rating (albeit on the low end): Baa3, Moody’s; BBB-, S&P.

Profitability is robust. Return on equity has averaged 20.1% over the last five years, while net margin has averaged 14.5%. These are very solid numbers, indicating strong margins and high returns on capital. This is a terrific business in a terrific space. And with economies of scale, switching costs, a reputation for quality/reliability regarding mission-critical products, IP, and a large installed base requiring replacement parts, the company does benefit from durable competitive advantages.

Of course, there are risks to consider. Regulation, litigation, and competition are omnipresent risks in every industry. Relative to other business models, however, I see all three as being somewhat diminished for Pentair. And regulation can actually help Pentair more than hurting it, as strict regulation regarding water treatment (such as new PFAS drinking water standards) is requiring massive municipal investments in water infrastructure (which will almost certainly be supportive of Pentair).

Being an international company, Pentair is exposed to geopolitics and currency exchange rates. Pentair can exhibit cyclicality, particularly in relation to infrastructure components in new builds. Nearly 40% of Pentair’s revenue comes from the pool segment, and spending on pools is highly discretionary. Pentair has long had a more diversified business model that could lean on different strengths at different times, but its major 2018 spin-off of nVent Electric has made Pentair much more concentrated as a pure-play water company.

Overall, I don’t consider Pentair to be an especially risky type of business, and that easier risk profile has long made it acceptable for the stock to command high multiples. However, a recent 20% pullback in the stock’s pricing has resulted in those multiples being fairly reasonable…

Valuation

The P/E ratio, based on adjusted EPS, is 18.4. That is not high for a water company reliably growing at HSD-LDD rates. This stock often commands a P/E ratio north of 25. The cash flow multiple of 17.2 is also very reasonable, and it’s below its own five-year average of 17.6. And the yield, as noted earlier, is in line with its own recent historical average. So the stock looks cheap when looking at basic valuation metrics. But how cheap might it be? What would a rational estimate of intrinsic value look like?

I valued shares using a two-stage dividend discount model analysis. I factored in a 10% discount rate, a 10-year dividend growth rate of 13%, and a long-term dividend growth rate of 8%. The near-term portion of the model reflects Pentair’s recent acceleration in the rate of its dividend growth, although I’m extrapolating that out in a way that assumes even more acceleration.

Seeing EPS growth where it’s at (both what’s been demonstrated and what’s forecasted ahead), and with the payout ratio being as low as it is, Pentair seems fully capable of doing a low-teens type of dividend growth rate over the next several years. I would then expect that to naturally slow down into a high-single-digit range befitting of a mature company.

The DDM analysis gives me a fair value of $88.89. The reason I use a dividend discount model analysis is because a business is ultimately equal to the sum of all the future cash flow it can provide. The DDM analysis is a tailored version of the discounted cash flow model analysis, as it simply substitutes dividends and dividend growth for cash flow and growth.

It then discounts those future dividends back to the present day to account for the time value of money, since a dollar tomorrow is not worth the same amount as a dollar today. I find it to be a fairly accurate way to value dividend growth stocks. From my vantage point, this stock is, at worst, fairly valued. But we’ll now compare that valuation with where two professional stock analysis firms have come out at. This adds balance, depth, and perspective to our conclusion.

Morningstar, a leading and well-respected stock analysis firm, rates stocks on a 5-star system. 1 star would mean a stock is substantially overvalued; 5 stars would mean a stock is substantially undervalued. 3 stars would indicate roughly fair value. Morningstar rates PNR as a 3-star stock, with a fair value estimate of $97.00.

CFRA is another professional analysis firm, and I like to compare my valuation opinion to theirs to see if I’m out of line. They similarly rate stocks on a 1-5 star scale, with 1 star meaning a stock is a strong sell and 5 stars meaning a stock is a strong buy. 3 stars is a hold. CFRA rates PNR as a 4-star “BUY” with a 12-month target price of $130.00.

I’m surprised to see that I came out so low. Averaging the three numbers out gives us a final valuation of $105.30, which would indicate the stock is possibly 14% undervalued.

Bottom line: Pentair is a high-quality company operating in one of the most attractive areas of the global economy. It caters to humanity’s most precious resource and what is likely going to be the “liquid gold” of the coming century. With a market-beating yield, high-single-digit dividend growth, a low payout ratio, 50 consecutive years of dividend increases, and the potential that shares are 14% undervalued, this Dividend King could be one of the best ways for long-term dividend growth investors to play the secular growth theme in water.

Note from D&I: How safe is PNR's dividend? We ran the stock through Simply Safe Dividends, and as we go to press, its Dividend Safety Score is 89. Dividend Safety Scores range from 0 to 100. A score of 50 is average, 75 or higher is excellent, and 25 or lower is weak. With this in mind, PNR's dividend appears very safe with a very unlikely risk of being cut.

Disclosure: I have no position in PNR.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: PXJ: We Expect More Waiting And Seeing By CAPEXers
data.description:
The Invesco Oil & Gas Services ETF (PXJ) covers oilfield service stocks, so things like floating rigs, offshore and onshore drilling, subsea installation, and a host of related engineering and procurement companies that develop oil and gas assets. These stocks require structural increases in oil prices, as that is what motivates their customers who have oil reserves to make the necessary CAPEX to significantly grow their asset portfolios, though depletion is always a reason for them to look to new wells for resources. While there are definitely some elements of the crisis that may have introduced structural factors upping the oil prices, it is not definitively the case that the oil price cannot eventually come down to more normal levels. That is also what the oil futures curve seems to believe, with it pointing to as soon as September 2026, even after the latest news, as the point after which oil is expected to once again fall below $80 per barrel.

It depends on the outcomes of the ceasefire talks in the near term, which seem not to have yielded the desired results of getting Iran to open up logistics. The US has now declared moves that they are going to make due to the failure of any diplomatic headway:

  • Vessels paying the "toll" to Iran will be stopped by the US Navy in a blockade to go into effect Monday
  • The US Navy has already started and will continue to try to de-mine the Strait and has threatened sharp retaliation if they come under enemy fire while doing so
  • They will institute a blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, meaning specifically a blockade of ports of the "enemy state" (Iran), where ships coming or going from elsewhere would be allowed to pass through, for example, to Gulf states.

Depending on how credible these moves are, oil transit may be able to increase. However, there have been some aborted transits by vessels, so the uncertainty and possible military escalation should put a premium on oil once again, especially on spot rates. As far as the futures curve is concerned, mostly a normalisation by September seems to still be the market position.

We don't think investments in new assets will be accelerated until it's clear that Gulf assets will be more structurally stranded by the conflict. Developers of these assets are probably also looking at the oil futures curve and seeing the markets believe that prices will come down soon enough one way or another. The normalisation timeline ends before planned assets can be developed in typical petroleum engineering timelines. Therefore, we are not yet ready to see significant accelerations in results for oil services companies as a consequence of the war. Also, while the conflict is great for E&P, services stock may be losing Gulf business due to the war, or at least seeing delayed delivery milestones, which drive results, without benefiting from a step change in new mandates outside the Gulf. Time will tell, and it will depend on either the war persisting or the threat/fees being passed onto ships traveling the strait.

PXJ Breakdown

PXJ contains a lot of heavy industry players commanding fleets of vessels and infrastructure needed for deploying the tools and helping extract oil from onshore and offshore sources. We recently covered TechnipFMC (FTI) as an example. Some general comments on these businesses are that they benefit from route economics. More installations in more fields means more effective logistics for maintenance and also less downtime in moving heavy equipment between locations to perform functions. Therefore, CAPEX upstream to develop new assets and pay PXJ constituents not only immediately increases revenues but also can significantly lower unit costs. In a feast or famine business, there has been a lot to gain, and a lot of gain in oil services stocks, even a week or two after the start of the war. The possibility of needing to replace structurally lost supply in the Middle East, for example, is positive for a lot of oil services companies.

Bull Case

  • Enduring tolls of ships passing through Hormuz, especially in the quantum so far enforced, could seriously put a floor on the oil price. Though maybe the ordered blockade will stop these tolls.
  • The possibility of the strait not being opened for quite a while longer could require investments elsewhere to develop supply in alternative geographies to the Gulf. This case becomes stronger as repeated attempts to broker a deal to open up the Strait fail, as it just has again, where, without diplomatic headway, now the US is taking a military stance in the Strait with a blockade. Though we must mention that the blockade is just on Iranian ports.
  • Perhaps the introduction of a premium on oil due to the perception of more habitual oil supply attacks and crises occurring around the globe would also put a floor on oil prices. The economic impact on oil prices would be through things like insurance costs rising.
  • Any structural increase in oil prices could spur significant demand from customers.
  • Any structural removal or effective removal of Gulf supply from markets could spur alternative geographies in developing oil assets to claim share.

Bear Case

  • If the war sees an enduring end, the bull trade in oil services and oil stocks in general could suddenly evaporate. The oil futures curve still seems to believe that a normalisation is coming as soon as in September 2026, where it is expected that the price will come below $80 per barrel. With normalisation expected soon based on current oil futures curve dynamics, we don't believe it is likely that a significant acceleration in development as a consequence of the war will happen.
  • Even if the war doesn't end, there may be existing, not fully utilised OPEC+ supply (outside the Gulf) that will come online, particularly as the OPEC+ countries were just in the middle of the process of phasing out supply cuts, which they could now accelerate - it would be something, though not enough on its own.
  • Also, it seems the traffic through Hormuz, even under the conflict, is not as bad as thought. Moreover, the promised blockade, which is also supposed to include de-mining efforts, if credible and successful, could permit transit of tankers working with allied Gulf states, even if, for the moment, transits are being aborted. Traffic will be allowed to pass through if not dealing with Iran; that would be the point of the blockade by the US Navy, as well as eliminating the tolls Iran is taking on transiting ships, which put a high floor on oil prices until now.

Bottom Line

Enforcement of the millions in tolls by Iran, which significantly (perhaps even as much as doubles) the cost of a journey so much as to put a floor on oil prices even if the war ends. Though it seems the US wants to eliminate this possibility specifically with a blockade, which could either increase conflict and operational risks, reducing supply, or successfully permit the transit of tankers to and from strangled Gulf states, thereby increasing supply. Hard to predict.

Still, it takes a lot to see an acceleration in heavy outlays in the industry and therefore in PXJ constituent mandates, especially with the oil futures curve suggesting normalisation sooner rather than later compared to the typical petroleum engineering timelines - perhaps reflecting that a blockade isn't deleterious to an eventual resolution of the Strait issue even if raising immediate conflict risk. Despite all that, there is also around 2 mbpd from non-Gulf states that have excess capacity that was being brought back with supply cut phaseouts, as well as around 2 mbpd that can leave the Gulf through pipelines. There could be some ways to recover supplies and reduce oil prices even if the blockade isn't successful or if the war doesn't end.

Another thing to consider is that a lot of oil and gas development happens in the Gulf States, meaning services stocks have big customers in this region. These projects cannot be carried out right now. So even if a higher oil price is good for E&P stock (as in not oil services stocks) in other regions, it may not be high for long enough to spur those other regions into higher CAPEX to offset potentially lost business in the Gulf for the oil services companies. Business may also slow down or be lost in other places in the Middle East that depend on Gulf logistics, such as in Iraq.

PXJ also has a higher expense ratio of 0.67% against the 0.38% of IEO, which is an oil E&P portfolio, and IEZ, which is closer as an oil equipment and services company, but very heavily skewed towards SLB (SLB) and Baker Hughes (BKR) to the point that there should be almost no fee at all, with the top two holdings enough to dictate the IEZ activity, being almost 50% of the allocation. The XES is even lower at 0.35%. The services ETFs aren't all that different; they contain some of the same stocks, like FTI, Noble Corp (NE), Baker Hughes, in similar exposures, around 4-5% each. XES, with the lowest expense ratio, has also had the best performance.

They are quite correlated too, so the difference in expense ratio doesn't seem that justified given that each captures a similar market factor.

data.title: Venture Global: Iran War Helps Bridge Two Soft Years Before Growth Shoots
data.description:
I have covered Venture Global, Inc. (VG) before, where I outlined the company’s background in detail and explained why I considered it an attractive buy despite some risks. Those risks materialized when BP (BP) won the arbitration case. The main problem is not only the possible fine itself that could reach more than $3.7 billion, but that Venture Global business model was based on selling as much spot cargos as possible before the plants were commissioned. This is the main reason why 2026 guidance is below 2025 figures, as I will discuss later.

In the future, I expect the company to be limited in this regard or clients demand clearer terms. However, due to the Iran war and the attack of the Qatar LNG facilities, the stock reached my previous fair value, but I am raising it to $20 per share as I believe the company could provide an attractive return for long term investors. Despite this, in the short term there could be turbulence, as 2027 will be a soft year and I expect the company to move lower when the Iran war finishes.

Background

Venture Global is an American producer and exporter of LNG, with the intention of becoming the largest U.S. LNG exporter, surpassing Cheniere Energy (LNG).

The company has three plants. Calcasieu Pass is the first operational LNG liquefaction and export facility with a 11.2 MTPA capacity.

Plaquemines is the second export facility and is under commissioning, with Phase I COD expected in Q4 2026 and Phase II COD in mid 2027. It will have a combined capacity of 28 MTPA plus a bolt-on of 6.4 MTPA with an expected FID in the second half of 2027.

Finally, the third plant, CP2, will have a total capacity of 29 MPTA and is already in construction with first LNG expected in the second half of 2027 and the FID for the Phase II expected in the next few weeks.

The company expects to have 81 MTPA capacity by 2030 and be able to operate above nameplate capacity up to 105 MTPA.

Most of this capacity, 49 MTPA so far, is sold under very long term contracts. Those contracts are mostly 20 years long and secure a huge $134 billion backlog.

The company does not start to fulfill the long-term contracts until the plants does not reach COD. This has propelled the litigations as customers argue that Venture Global was delaying the COD to sell the cargos on the spot market (where they had a much higher fee). Fees for long term contracts are around $2/MMBTu while on the spot cargos are around $6/ MMBTu.

While Venture Global lost the BP arbitration, it has won the Repsol and Shell ones, has recently settled the Edison one, and now just the Galp one is remaining.

However, I expect this could shorten the times to reach the remaining CODs, decreasing the cargos the company can sell into the spot market.

Despite this, is important to note that management has met the announced timelines so far and has delivered on their commitments:

Financial Position and Valuation

Venture Global is highly indebted, as it is still building its plants and doesn’t benefit from all its cashflows. At the end of Q4, the company had $3.4 billion in cash and $35 billion in debt, which will further increase until CP2 is completed. I expect debt will increase by about $6 billion in 2026 and 2027. From 2028 I expect a significant reduction in debt increase; however, I have accounted for a $3 billion payment for the BP litigation.

Venture Global revenue depends mostly on two fees, the liquefaction fee and the commodity fee as explained in its 10-K:

The LNG sales price structure under our Contracted SPAs generally includes (i) a fixed liquefaction fee, a portion of which is subject to an annual adjustment for inflation; (ii) a variable commodity fee equal to at least 115% of Henry Hub per MMBtu of LNG; and (iii) a transportation charge, if sold on a DPU basis.

This is important because the liquefaction fee is fixed in the long term contracts and much lower than the cargoes sold on the spot market (hence the reduction from 2024 to 2025 as Calcesieu Pass was commissioned). For long term contracts the liquefaction fee is around $2/MMBtu while on the spot could be above $6/MMBtu. However, the commodity fee is based on Henry Hub price plus a margin. As more export capacity comes online, I expect Henry Hub to increase and Venture Global to earn a higher margin.

To calculate EBITDA I have used the following assumptions based on the expansion capacity of the different plants and expected volumes on long term contracts:

I believe that 2026 and 2027 commodity fee estimates are conservative, as I expect that, given the Iran war and the attacks on Qatar LNG, the spot cargos could be sold higher than in 2025. However, I prefer to be on the conservative side as I am already above management guidance provided before those attacks.

The EBITDA reduction in 2026 compared to 2025 is because the company expects a reduction in the liquefaction fee for both Calcasieu pass, as it will be already fully commissioned, and Plaquemines, which will achieve Phase I commission during the year. As mentioned before, when a plant is commissioned the long term contracts start to take effect.

However, due to the Iran war I expect Venture Global will beat guidance. Taking my numbers into consideration, these are my EBITDA estimates:

I expect a softer 2027 than 2026 because despite higher volumes in 2027, Calcasieu pass and Plaquemines will be both already commissioned (earning lower fees) and CP2 will have only a few cargoes.

From there, volumes will rapidly increase driving EBITDA and earnings higher despite lower fees due to long term contracts. I believe that with those long-term contracts fully operational and once the legal issues are resolved, the company will be valued higher and I consider a 11.5x EV/EBITDA reasonable.

With that valuation, its clear investors should have a long-term vision on the company as 2027 will be soft; however, in the longer term there is value.

As those price targets are a few years out, I have discounted them at a 10%. With that, I believe the current fair value could be around $20 per share, and 2030 price could be above $30 per share, providing about a 25% annualized return.

Risks

Although Venture Global’s long-term fundamentals remain solid, there could be several near-term risks.

The first one is that the stock has surged more than 30% since the Iran war began; however, while the company will benefit with its spot cargoes, the benefit will be limited as it is already working at maximum capacity. When the war finishes, I expect the stock will experience a pullback.

Another risk is the BP arbitration case, the final payment is unknown and could move the market; however, I expect the impact to be limited.

Leverage also remains elevated, and I expect net debt to reach $40 billion before any deleveraging phase begins. If credit markets are closed when the company need financing, it may need to delay some projects, delaying the EBITDA growth.

Overall, while long term visibility remains high, 2026 and 2027 will be soft years as the company will have less spot cargoes which could bring volatility to the stock.

Conclusion

Venture Global is poised to experience a significant growth from 2028 until beyond 2030 when all its three plants will be commissioned. I believe this growth is not priced in.

However, the recent surge due to the Iran war could provide some short term stock volatility as benefits from that disruption won’t be immediate and 2026 and 2027 will be softer years.

Despite this I consider the current stock price attractive as my current fair value is $20 per share, representing more than a 50% upside and I expect the stock to trade above $30 in a few years, providing a possible 25% annualized return.
