data.title: Your Favorite BDCs Might Be More Leveraged Than You Think
data.description:
BDC investing, by definition, comes with above-average risks.

The role that BDCs play in the marketplace is to finance small and mid-sized private businesses that can't access capital from traditional (or less expensive) sources such as banks and public debt markets. They must invest at least 70% of their assets in U.S. businesses and provide management (strategic and financial) support for them. Plus, to qualify for tax benefits, BDCs have to also distribute at least 90% of their income to shareholders (similar concept as for REITs).

The reason why these businesses that BDCs finance can't tap into the more borrower-friendly sources is because of either weak financials or complex (risk-saturated) transactions that they seek to fund (e.g., inorganic growth funding, debt refinancings, leveraged buyouts, specific business unit carve-outs, etc.)

These deals are inherently underpinned by relatively high leverage.

As we know, leverage works both ways: it juices the upside when things are good and compounds the downside when they are bad.

And this is one of the key reasons why the BDC (BIZD) space is so cyclical.

The past 3 years when the S&P 500 (SPY) was booming were characterized by strong BDC total returns. Now, when the market has headed in the opposite direction, the BDC sector has completely gone out of vogue.

All this is very logical.

Just think of it (a couple of rhetorical questions):

  • In the case of recession, what are the odds of highly leveraged companies that cannot access traditional bank financing avoiding any defaults?
  • How defensive can these BDC underlying portfolios be if their cost of debt is ~10%?
  • What are the chances of certain BDCs remaining disciplined in the past 3 years when the markets were booming and new BDC funds were mushrooming (due to massive inflows of capital into private credit space)?

Now, one of the key ways to mitigate these risks, or the so-called value traps, is to cherry-pick conservatively leveraged BDCs. The presence of prudent leverage levels could very likely indicate the following:

  1. In case of increasing non-accruals, the effects won't be as severe as for aggressively leveraged peers.
  2. The management is probably risk-averse or rather very careful about risk assumption.
  3. The BDC has ample dry powder to go on offense when everyone else is battling with liquidity and balance sheet management issues.

Moreover, it is not so that by deciding to invest in prudently leveraged BDCs we are drastically limiting our current income streams.

For example, Blue Owl Technology Finance Corp. (OTF) and Fidus Investment (FDUS) are yielding 12.3% and 11.9%, respectively, while having debt-to-equity levels of 0.78x and 0.87x, respectively. The dividend yields are slightly lower than the sector average of 14.7%, but the debt side is much better (sector average is 1.25x).

Simply put, the benefits of venturing into conservatively leveraged BDCs are much greater than the negatives that might stem from not maximizing yields or not scooping the most discounted names.

However, in practice, it can be quite complicated to really understand the inherent leverage levels. Debt-to-equity ratio is critical, but it does not capture the entire perimeter of debt avenues.

In the article below, I would like to showcase 5 well-known BDCs and what their "full leverage levels" look like. I will also insert a sixth BDC, which is the worst case I am aware of.

The objective is not to recommend specific buying or selling opportunities, but rather to provide more data points based on which informed decisions could be made.

Plus, given that the approach can be applied to other BDCs as well, I hope that after reading this article some BDC investors will be better equipped to cherry-pick the most defensive names.

Hidden pockets of leverage

I have chosen a diversified blend of BDCs, which include some of the most well-known and largest externally managed BDCs as well as two internally managed ones, which have been my top picks for quite some time.

Ares Capital (ARCC), Blue Owl Capital (OBDC) and Sixth Street Specialty Lending (TSLX) are the three externally managed BDCs. Trinity Capital (TRIN) and Capital Southwest (CSWC) are two internally managed players, which typically are preferred by durable income investors (especially CSWC).

And as I mentioned above, I have also included PennantPark Investment Corporation (PNNT) to illustrate how bad things can go (i.e., how massive the hidden leverage amounts can become).

Now, in the article below, you will see a table with 5 columns (I'm not counting the "Ticker" column, which should be obvious). Let me provide a bit of context for each column.

D/E or debt-to-equity, is a simple leverage metric that is commonly used by analysts and reflected in BDC investor materials. It measures the level of BDC debt relative to the equity that is carried on the balance sheet.

The sector average is 1.25x, which is also the usual ceiling that BDCs don't want to breach in order to avoid flirting with excessive leverage and potentially breaching BDC regulatory requirements (D/E of 2x).

The unfunded column shows the outstanding commitments that a BDC has towards its portfolio companies. The percentages reflect the unfunded capital relative to Q4 2025 asset levels.

The thing here is that BDCs tend to provide loans in the form of credit facilities, which can be used to a certain amount depending on the portfolio company's needs. In some cases the picture gets more complicated when BDCs have other types of commitments. For example, OBDC has ~$0.6 billion of capital commitments to its sister BDCs that could be called in case there is a need for extra funding (in the environment when liquidity is leaving the BDC system, this is certainly not a low-probability scenario).

While these are commitments, again, given the prevailing headwinds and system-wide redemptions, we are likely to see an increasing utilization of the available funding. The fact that new loans have become more expensive supports this assumption as well.

Adjusted D/E is the implied capital structure based on the full usage of the unfunded commitments.

JV income, or joint venture income, stems from cash flows that are generated by unconsolidated JVs. The percentages reflect a portion of total interest income.

The rationale for BDC partnering with other capital providers is to grow the asset base without introducing significant pressures on the consolidated capital structure (this is what we see through D/E ratios).

In the situation when the sector average D/E is 1.25x - right at the typical ceiling that BDCs avoid breaching - and considering exhausted NII payout ratios (e.g., sector average base dividend coverage is 97%; no capital available from retained earnings), it should not come as a surprise that BDCs are exploring "unconventional ways" to keep growing their portfolios.

Now, the big issue here is that these unconsolidated JVs are not subject to BDC regulatory requirements. Hence, BDCs can use aggressive leverage amounts to squeeze more juice out of each dollar deployed at the JV level.

JV D/E reflects how leveraged the underlying unconsolidated JVs are from which certain BDCs generate a notable amount of earnings.

Ticker D/E Unfunded Adjusted D/E JV income JV D/E
ARCC 1.12x 17.9% 1.32x 15.8% 4.05x
OBDC 1.26x 13.7% 1.43x 10.0% 4.01x
TSLX 1.08x 9.9% 1.18x N/A N/A
TRIN 1.18x 6.8% 1.26x N/A N/A
CSWC 1.07x 13.4% 1.21x N/A N/A
PNNT 1.57x 29.3% 2.03x 37.8% 13.1x

P.S.1. All data are based on 2025 10-K filings.

P.S.2. For PNNT, the fiscal calendar differs from the regular calendar; hence, the most recent 10-Q is used.

Key takeaways

From the analysis above, I have four important takeaways.

#1: The adjusted leverage levels (accounting for unfunded commitments) for almost all BDCs are greater than what are reported in the investor materials through standard D/E ratios.

For BDCs that are already operating with debt-saturated books, this is a bigger issue that investors have to recognize. For example, it reduces the capacity to grow and makes a BDC more vulnerable to rising non-accruals (e.g., if the portfolio companies start to struggle and thus tap into their credit facility to handle the shocks, the consequences if they fail could be quite massive for the BDC).

#2: This goes back to the point above of reduced growth capacity.

In the current environment, it comes in very handy to have a well-capitalized balance sheet. As the spreads have risen due to the capital leaving the system, the BDCs with ample "dry powder" could take advantage of it by leaning into higher-spread loan investments in a more selected fashion.

BDCs with tight debt levels don't possess this luxury.

#3: Unconsolidated JV income is prevalent for externally managed BDCs.

My table above shows how it works in practice. TRIN and CSWC don't have significant unconsolidated subsidiaries. It is interesting to see that TSLX (externally managed BDC) does not have them either. It just once again confirms that TSLX is a prudent BDC (the unfunded commitments and D/E levels are below average as well).

However, when it comes to TRIN and CSWC, both managers have joined forces by forming a JV (50% for CSWC and 50% for TRIN; a "funny" coincidence). Similarly, in Q4 2025, TSLX communicated that it will be launching a JV with Carlyle Secured Lending Inc. (CGBD) to keep the growth engine running.

Again, it goes well in line with what I communicated above: BDCs have relatively exhausted balance sheets, base dividend coverage levels are tight, and unconsolidated JVs are a viable way to secure growth.

#4: Unconsolidated JVs tend to be aggressively leveraged.

This point is rather obvious but very critical. Both ARCC and OBDC have assumed much greater leverage in their JVs, which clearly surpasses the BDC (consolidated) regulatory requirement of 2:1. This way the JV yields are nicely enhanced, but, of course, it all comes with much greater risk.

Finally, in the table I have included PNNT. It is highly leveraged on all fronts: from consolidated D/E, adjusted D/E (including unfunded commitments), and the JV income side. It shows how in practice things could get seriously leveraged up.

Back in December 2024, when I recognized these hidden risks, I issued a specific piece on it: PennantPark Investment: I've Never Seen So Complex And Speculative Structure

Up until early 2026, PNNT performed largely in line with the sector, but as soon as the tide started to go out, this is what happened:

It goes back to what I said earlier:

Leverage works both ways: it juices the upside when things are good and compounds the downside when they are bad.

Final remarks

This analysis has not changed my views on my top BDC picks, such as CSWC, TRIN, and TSLX. If anything, it has just enhanced my confidence levels about these picks and once again confirmed the benefit of paying extra P/NAV instead of skewing my portfolio to the deeply discounted BDCs.

However, I think that there is one big conclusion that we can make, which is that internally managed BDCs are not just about avoiding base management and incentive fees that are relevant for externally managed peers.

Instead, it is about shareholder-friendly incentives as well.

One way I'm reading the fact that some externally managed BDCs have assumed aggressively leveraged unconsolidated JV exposures is their inherent motivation to earn fees, which are linked to asset size and income generation above certain hurdle rates. Adding leverage definitely helps both components.

In a nutshell, for durable income and resilient NAV, my recommendation to lean into high-quality, internally managed BDCs remains intact.

data.title: Can Trump Actually Blockade The Strait Of Hormuz?
data.description:
Effective immediately, the United States Navy, the Finest in the World, will begin the process of BLOCKADING any and all Ships trying to enter, or leave, the Strait of Hormuz.- President Donald Trump

Written by Sam Kovacs

Introduction

You've read the news. Following 21-hour-long negotiations between VP JD Vance and Iran in Islamabad, which ultimately collapsed, Trump announced a full naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz.

This, of course, is bullish for oil and bearish for the rest, as it significantly increases risk in the region and would lock in ALL Middle Eastern oil behind the strait.

The questions I have and will address in this article are:

  • Can the US physically blockade the strait?
  • What are the real challenges to a blockade?
  • Will Trump actually follow through?

And how to position as a consequence.

The US has the force to block the strait.

Look, let's get this out of the way. The US can absolutely physically block the Strait of Hormuz. Hormuz is a 6-mile-wide funnel. The US has more than enough force in theater to get this done:

  • 3 carrier strike groups: USS Abraham Lincoln in the Arabian Sea, USS Gerald R. Ford, and USS George H.W. Bush en route.
  • It has 6 independent destroyers in the Arabian Sea, of which 2 transited the strait on Saturday. This was the first time since the beginning of this conflict that US warships have passed the strait.
  • It has marine teams that can board non-compliant vessels.
  • It has submarines that have already sunk an Iranian warship.
  • It has 200+ aircraft total and 30+ warships in all. This is the largest US naval presence in the Middle East since 2003.

A single destroyer can track the entire width of the strait. Shipping lanes are no more than a couple miles wide each, and with traffic close to zero, there is nothing preventing the US Navy from doing this today.

If there is one area where you will not compete with the U.S. of A is conventional warfare.

So can the US physically blockade Hormuz?

Yes. No questions asked.

Will the enforcement be total?

This is where it gets a little bit more complicated. When you think of a US blockade of Hormuz, you must ask: who would be blocked?

That would be countries that are non-aligned with the US, and on friendly enough terms with Iran, among which are Iranian-flagged ships, as well as India and China.

Now the US can absolutely blockade the Iranian-flagged ships; Iran no longer has the navy to contest this.

All commercial ships will stop and turn around. No captain is stupid enough to challenge an order coming from a US destroyer. If they were hell-bent on it, Marines boarding the boat would put an end to their little venture.

India will likely stomp their feet and contest vocally, as they are trying to assert themselves as a non-aligned country, but in reality would have no choice but to comply.

The wildcard is...you guessed it, China. This is 100% of the game in a blockade scenario.

China imports about 1.5mn bp/d of Iranian crude. Of the limited number of ships that have been allowed to move through the Strait, most have been Chinese.

They have deals with Tehran.

If the US blockades China and boards vessels sailing under their flag, it would move to a confrontation between the two mega powers. Interestingly enough, it would be a clear confrontation over freedom of navigation, the very principle that the US has championed for the past century.

The difference is that the geopolitical status quo that existed between 1989 and 2009 is no longer in play, despite many hanging onto that historical oddity.

The most likely outcome is that Trump doesn't want confrontation with China, and that they might let a few Chinese ones slip.

Claiming a total blockade and implementing a partial one.

It seems to be that in this day and age, the guiding principle is that you do not let the truth get in the way of a good story.

What's more, there is a chance of this getting dirty. If Iran wants to contest the US blockade, they still have mines in their inventory, small boats capable of laying mines, midget submarines, and degraded but still present anti-ship cruise missiles.

What is interesting is that the US said it wanted to demine. It has very limited MCM capabilities, boats that were not even able to accurately identify mines in California's clear waters in testing. They are unproven in Hormuz's murky waters.

Plus, mines are an asset in a total blockade, not a liability. And even then, removing mines without a total mine map is a nightmare.

The credibility index

The problem, as this war progresses, is that the BSD (Big Swinging Donald) claims are getting harder and harder to take at face value.

Let's take a look at what has happened so far.

  • March 10: He said "consequences at a level never seen before" if mines are placed. The US destroyed a few minelayers but destroyed no mines. Iran retained 90% of its mine-laying capabilities.
  • March 11: Navy will escort tankers "as soon as possible". Navy refused and said it would be impossible as the risks were too high.
  • March 21: "48-hour ultimatum" to open Hormuz. The power plants were never hit, but the deadline was pushed back.
  • April 1: "No ceasefire until Hormuz is open, free, and clear". 6 days later he agreed to a ceasefire, but no Hormuz open.
  • April 7: "a whole civilization will die tonight". Announced a ceasefire 90 minutes before deadline.
  • April 9: "all military objectives met". Same regime in power, Hormuz closed, The nuclear program is still in the books.
  • April 12: "Blockade effective immediately".

So what will happen?

Difficult to say, but all I know is I can only tell my wife so many times that I will most definitely bring those items to the dry cleaners before she stops trusting me.

How much more leeway does a president making claims get?

The pattern is clear. None of the ultimatums saw any follow through since the beginning of the conflict.

Deadlines were pushed. Immediate becomes maybe, later, sometimes never. The Navy operates using its own assessments of risk rather than moving based on the President's claims.

It's politically touchy.

Like I said above, forbidding Chinese tankers risks an escalation which the US neither wants or needs. This is the wildcard and China might get its own side-letter deal.

The US Gulf allies want a strait open, not another lock with a full blockade. It doesn't make the situation any worse for them (except Iraq who had authorizations to export), but it doesn't do anything to solve anything.

Domestic pressure will continue to build, as Brent went from $60s to $118 in Q1, which was the largest increase since 1988. The midterms might slip if inflation comes through.

Anyways its coming, as I laid out in my previous article, we haven't even seen the second order transmission yet, which is coming regardless of what oil does in the next few weeks. But a blockade worsens and accelerates this.

Iran has said that a blockade would be a breach of the ceasefire.

My take on the ceasefire, is that both parties were happy to take a breather rather than have to follow through on worse case escalations on civilian infrastructure.

They signed a ceasefire, whilst neither agreed on a path to resolving the war, nor even by agreeing on the terms of the ceasefire: US wanted Hormuz open (impossible with mines). Iran remained control. Iran wanted ceasefire in Lebanon. Israel continued onslaught. Iran also attacked the Saudi East-West pipeline after the ceasefire.

Can the ceasefire even last until April 12th? The answer is no.

What to expect in markets.

Markets have been trying to look past the war in the past two weeks. Monday will be a big test to this.

As we're just two hours away from the Sunday night opening of Brent in London, the default is likely that oil gaps up, and the markets are dragged back into the narrative. Maybe they shrug it off somewhat Monday. If they do it's a sign only a re-escalation can break the momentum.

A big red day in the S&P 500, would suggest we're not out of the woods and the market is headed for lower lows in the next few weeks.

Longer term, the inflation/stagflation book remains the way to play the current situation. Atlantic Oil companies, REITs with escalators, businesses with real durable pricing power.

It's not an environment to get cute. We've made some defensive rotations within our portfolios and continue to position dynamically as and when it makes sense.

data.title: Corning Looks Overvalued, Until You Drill Down And See All That's Here - Buy
data.description:
I understand why Corning (GLW) management talks so much about its Springboard initiative.

This multi-year program, launched in early 2024, aims to focus investors (and, perhaps, employees) on the extent to which the company is growing business.

Toward that end, management has been disclosing revenue targets, and periodically revising them upward.

To be honest, and with all due respect for what management is trying to convey, presentations like that put me to sleep. All, or at least many, companies articulate plans like that — “XYZ company plans to grow — More at 6 o’clock!”

A journalist would call that a dog-bite-man non-news story.

I prefer to look for the proverbial man-bites-dog bona fide news stories. In the context of growth plans, this would discuss how growth efforts actually fared. And more importantly, it would discuss what’s driving growth.

Numbers are a starting point. For GLW, their messages are ok.

But I already get that from some parts of the growth-rate tables I’ll show below. And the upward trending Seeking Alpha Annual and Quarterly Estimates Revisions presentations say much. Based on those sources, I’m willing to accept that GLW is achieving at least as much as Springboard aims to deliver.

But those numbers live in the past. And they only tell us “What?” If I were to stop here, I’d say GLW shares are overvalued. And at best, I’d rate the stock as a “Hold.”

But drilling down into GLW’s business paints a different — and much more exciting — picture. It answers the “Why?” questions. And in my opinion, those answers justify enough optimism about GLW’s future to easily support a “Buy” rating.

So let’s get to it.

Setting The Stage

GLW, founded in 1851, is ancient relative to other tech and AI plays (which is what it is). Actually, it’s ancient relative to most of the companies in which many of us invest today.

And through it all, it’s been a glassmaker.

When GLW launched, glass was typically crafted in workshops. From the start, it saw R&D as the key to distinguishing itself from its many rivals. So as it deepened its “knowledge of the makeup, chemistry and physics of glass,” it pursued then-unconventional uses for it.

In 1877, it introduced a special glass designed for railroad signal lanterns.

In 1879, it created a glass enclosure for Thomas Edison’s new incandescent light bulb.

In 1915, it launched PYREX, an especially durable glass for laboratory use.

And then, to borrow a tag line from a different source (the Energizer Bunny), Corning kept going and going and going.

And all along the way, GLW continued to become increasingly intertwined with tech as it evolved. (Hence this glass company’s ticker, which evokes a sense of glowing.)

Here are quick back-of-the-envelope notes on the company‘s current operating units.

  • Optical Communications: Optical fiber and related things for use in communications — It’s important now in AI and data-centers.
  • Displays: Precision glass for flat-panel displays used in TVs, laptops, mobile devices, monitors, etc.
  • Specialty Materials: Super-strong Gorilla Glass known best for use in mobile phones; GLW glass also used in aerospace, semiconductors, and to shield from radiation.
  • Automotive: Used in catalytic converters and in other parts of vehicles to boost strength and reduce weight.
  • Life Sciences: Glass for products used in research labs.
  • Hemlock and Emerging Growth Businesses: Semiconductor and solar opportunities.

Don’t worry about information overload. I’m not going to cover all those groups equally. I’ll focus most on where the biggest opportunities are. I’m sure you guessed that Optical is going to get a lot of attention. It’s about AI.

Anyway, the segment tables below show you how the revenues and profits are divided and where the growth has been.

Yes, we see that Optical is the most important area. So let’s get to it…

Optical, The Star Of This Show

Superficially, we can say prospects for fibre optic cables are great. That’s based on data centers needing more and more and more of these in order to accommodate the needs of AI and HPC (High Performance Computing).

But the relationship between more data center capacity and more business for GLW is just a starting point. Digging deeper, we’ll see opportunities for GLW Optical to expand well beyond the opportunities it currently enjoys.

We must view data center connectivity in three parts.

(1) Scale Out

This, today, is GLW’s bread and butter. It’s what’s driving the super growth we’re now seeing for Optical.

It refers to connecting GPU clusters within a data center to one another. This isn’t like connecting devices within one’s home or office. It can involve superclusters of hundreds of thousands of GPUs spanning potentially hundreds of meters of data center floor space.

Scale-out connectivity needs to be breathtakingly fast because this is how AI inference, the tasks we’re constantly doing and wanting done now, takes place. Speed and signal clarity are mandatory.

Copper connections aren’t great for this sort of thing. They’re too slow. Signal degradation becomes troublesome as distance grows.

Hence glass-based fibre optics is preferred here over copper.

Adding spice is the potential for especially large deals.

Earlier this year, GLW and Meta Platforms (META) announced a multi-year up-to-$6 billion deal to “accelerate the buildout of the most advanced data centers in the United States to support Meta’s apps, technologies, and AI ambitions.”

During the 1/28/26 earnings call, CEO Wendell Weeks added:

We are concluding similar long-term agreements with other major customers to dedicate capacity for them as well.

This is the most widely discussed aspect of GLW Optical.

Whether the potential for other big META-like deals is reflected in the stock’s valuation is up for debate. Assuming, for the sake of argument it is, let’s move now to two other less-discussed aspects of Optical.

(2) Scale Up

From the big distances handled by scale-out, let’s now compress.

Scale-up refers to connectivity within a single GPU cluster. The aim is to treat multiple GPUs as if they were a singular object. We want them to share memory and to combine efforts on a singular task.

This is a heck of a lot more intimate than the scale-out type of connections.

To a lay person, scale up would seem much simpler than scaling out - less distance means less challenge.

Not so!

Tightly compressed spacing presents a whole different set of difficulties. Here, even the most seemingly trivial degree of latency becomes a critical no-no.

Copper won’t go away from here. But it needs a helping hand.

Enter CPO (Co-Packaged Optics).

Skipping the overpowering amount of technical jargon and details, this involves bringing GLW’s fibre optics directly into the chip clusters to create better, more perfect, if you will, connections than copper alone can deliver in these tight spaces.

The current approach, plugging separate transceiver modules into the front of a network switch, works today. But the industry is already developing the next generation: Co-Packaged Optics (CPO), where lasers and light guides are built directly onto the chip itself.

CPO promises 70% power savings for AI workloads compared to traditional pluggable optics. Broadcom forecasts that CPO will reach the energy efficiency needed for mass deployment (below 10 picojoules per bit) by 2028, with advanced versions hitting 5 pJ/bit by 2029.

Large-scale CPO deployment is expected between 2028 and 2030. The CPO market is projected to exceed $20 billion by 2036, growing at 37% annually from 2026. This means the photonics opportunity doesn’t peak in 2027, it accelerates into the next decade.

(Source.)

Broadcom (AVGO) CEO Hok Tan created a fuss in March 2026, when he supposedly downplayed fibre in this context. Actually, though, Tan was talking about “when,” not “if.” Tan’s thoughts on timing line up well with the above quote. And in fact, GLW and AVGO are working together on CPO.

All in all, scale out was and is GLW’s past and present big thing. Scale up (and CPO) will be GLW’s next big thing.

This isn’t the sort of thing that can be retrofitted into old racks. But it will eventually be part of new builds. And it puts GLW into an aspect of the market in which it has not previously participated. So news data centers will, once cPO kicks in, get more GLW product per center.

(3) Scale Across

Think of this as scale-out on steroids.

Scale out means connecting GPU clusters within a data center and getting them to function as one. Scale across kicks it up a notch by connecting clusters across data centers or buildings across a campus or metro region. Here, too, the goal is to get everything to function as one.

This is the next wave.

Due to limitations such as power, cooling, and land, scale-up/out alone cannot expand clusters indefinitely. Data centers have limited power supply capacity, and adding GPUs will cause system outages. Air conditioning and liquid cooling also incur significant costs.

These challenges have given rise to Scale-Across. Based on Spectrum-XGS Ethernet technology, it connects geographically dispersed data centers through a high-performance network, enabling them to operate as a unified computing resource pool, completely overcoming the physical limitations of a single data center.

...

Scale-Across is not a simple expansion, but a paradigm shift in AI infrastructure. It breaks the physical boundaries of data centers through collaborative innovation of optical fibers, optical transceivers, and switches, laying the foundation for the next wave of AI applications. As this technology matures and becomes more widespread, we will witness the emergence of AI superfactories with millions of GPUs and billions of watts of power consumption, supporting cutting-edge scenarios such as physical AI (such as robotics and autonomous driving), personalized healthcare, and national-level language services.

(Source)

This will create a market for GLW that is distinct from the ones it now serves:

Finally, scaling across is about connecting multiple data centers or AI clusters to create a distributed network. As AI applications become more widespread, the need for interconnection between geographically dispersed facilities grows. For example, a company may need to link its primary data center in one city to another primary data center in another region to build an even larger AI cluster.

High density fiber optic cables are essential tools for this type of scaling. As data center operators and carriers deploy conduits with limited space, maximizing fiber density is key. Corning’s high fiber count cable portfolio, like the Contour™ Flow cable, are designed for interconnection deployments, supporting the scalability of AI networks across regions and use cases.

(Source)

The Supporting Cast

There’s a good deal of cyclicality in these (non-Optics) areas. So don’t expect great things across the board here.

Nevertheless, these businesses are important. Just as performers in supporting roles can do wonders for films, etc., we should respect the likelihood that some of GLW’s supporting players can contribute nicely to an investment case for the stock.

Most of the supporting players contribute by serving as cash cows. But right now, two of them have exciting secular prospects.

(1) Specialty Materials — Glass

Foldable cell phones, which now exist, may gain popularity as more vendors, including Apple (APPL) introduce them. That could accelerate the next upgrade cycle, and boost demand for Gorilla Glass adapted to this protocol.

But an especially huge opportunity is looming in semiconductor substrates.

A substrate, usually silicon,

is essentially a supporting component upon which or within which the elements of a semiconductor device are fabricated or attached.

But AI and HPC are to be demanding better. And GLW is rising to the challenge. This may become the next big thing in substrates. That reflects

growing recognition that glass substrates can deliver up to 40% speed improvements while reducing power consumption by half compared to conventional organic substrates.

The surge in AI accelerators and HPC devices has created unprecedented demands for bandwidth density and power delivery that traditional organic substrates simply cannot support.

(Source)

(2) Hemlock — Solar Opportunity

GLW won’t be making solar cells. But it will work with key inputs, ingots and wafers.

A silicon ingot is the bulk form of crystalline silicon before it is thinly sliced into wafers. A high speed wire saw with diamond blades slices the ingot into round wafers about 300 to 1000 microns in thickness, and 25 mm to 300 mm in diameter. These wafers are used in solar cells in solar panels.

(Source)

President Trump’s hostility to Biden-era legislative initiatives might suggest solar isn’t hip right now.

But oil markets have become unstable. And U.S. policymakers might get increasingly uncomfortable with China’s commanding lead in solar, an anathema to the current administration. Also:

Utility-scale solar PV has achieved a 90% cost reduction since 2010, making it the cheapest source of new electricity generation in most parts of the world.

So it pays to consider that today’s anti-green conventional wisdom probably won’t persist forever.

Meanwhile, on 10/29/25, Solar Power World wrote:

With incredible speed, Corning announced in its Q3 earnings call that it has brought its Michigan silicon ingot and wafer factory online. The plans for the solar factory were first publicly revealed in October 2024. The facility is on Corning’s Hemlock semiconductor campus, and Hemlock polysilicon will feed to Corning’s new wafer factory.

It seems I’m not alone in thinking solar still has a future. The same source also told us:

The company activated idle solar polysilicon assets at the Hemlock plant and now has more than 80% of its polysilicon and wafer capacity committed for the next five years.

Also:

Per a report from Mordor Intelligence, the U.S. Solar energy market is expected to witness a compound annual growth rate of 12.34% between 2025 and 2030. The entry to the Solar industry for Corning is of high return and low risk. From its Solar Market Access Platform, the company is expected to generate $2.5 billion in revenues from this new MAP by 2028, with a positive impact on profit and cash flow.

Contributing To GLW’s Valuation

The paucity of discussion I’m seeing relating to what’s going on in Specialty Materials and Hemlock contributes to the investment case I present today.

As with the details of the full range of what GLW is doing in Optical, I believe further attention here would diminish complaints we see about GLW’s valuation.

GLW’s Numbers

This section will look ok, but not necessarily spectacular. I’m not bullish on GLW because of what we’ll see here. I’m bullish because of how I see the company evolving, as discussed above. That said…

Let’s start with the quarterly Revenue and EPS trends.

I have no complaints about what we’re seeing here.

Let’s move on to historic and forward growth rates.

(I compare GLW to medians among companies in the electronic components industry and in the portfolio of the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY). I use medians since these aren’t impacted by wild distortions often caused by unusual data items.)

The 3Y historical numbers are yawn-worthy. But the YOY and FWD figures look pretty darn good. Those likely reflect GLW’s leaning harder into hot businesses, the kinds of things management has probably been factoring into its springboard targets.

Here are the core fundamentals.

I’d characterize these numbers as adequate. I wish they were better. But I’m glad they aren’t worse.

The best part of the table is the lower portion addressing returns on assets and equity. GLW’s numbers are in line with those of the SPY universe. But they’re meaningfully better than the industry medians.

The four upper rows, relating to debt don’t impress at first sight. But they’re no that bad. I’ll address this further in the Risk section below.

Meanwhile, here are the Financial Statement Highlights.

Same story. Not great. Not awful.

Risk

Given the importance to GLW of data center work, it’s important to say that I do not believe in the data center bubble fears. (See here.)

As to GLW’s financial risk, CFO Edward Schlesinger delivered some good news on the 1/28/26 earnings call (emphasis added):

As we invest, we will use a variety of tools to share the cost and risk with our customers, including customer prepayments and stringent long-term customer commitments to ensure we generate strong returns on our investments and secure our planned cash flows. We also seek to maintain a strong and efficient balance sheet. We're in great shape. We have one of the longest debt tenors in the S&P 500. Our current average debt maturity is about 21 years, and we have no significant debt coming due in any given year.

Finally, we expect to continue our strong track record of returning excess cash to shareholders. We already have a strong dividend. Therefore, as we go forward, our primary vehicle for returning excess cash to shareholders will be share buybacks. We have an excellent track record. Over the last decade, we repurchased 800 million shares, close to a 50% reduction in our outstanding shares.

Folks, GLW operates in capital-intensive businesses. So it must keep the capex spigot open. We saw that in the Financial Statement Highlights table above.

But that table also showed indications of restraint.

The company hasn’t been acquiring lately. And it doesn’t seem inclined to let the dividend payout ratio stay as high as it went in 2024. Schlesinger’s expressed preference for rewarding shareholders via stock buybacks, which can be timed in a discretionary way, makes sense.

While one may wish for lower debt ratios, the foregoing, coupled with long debt maturities, makes it clear that management has a firm grasp on the steering wheel and its eyes firmly focused on the road.

So, all in all, I’d say GLW’s risk is about average by growth stock standards (and especially relative to other AI plays).

What to Do About GLW Stock

Here are GLW’s valuation ratios.

Yes, they’re high. There’s no doubt about that.

Since mid-2025, most Seeking Alpha analysts have been neutral. (The formal SA Analyst Rating is at “Hold.”)

Many of the Seeking Alpha headlines suggest valuation is what’s keeping the analysts from being bullish. And on 4/10/26, one analyst issued a “Sell” rating accompanied by a headline telling readers not to value GLW “Like A Hot Tech Stock.”

Actually, I’m saying you should value GLW like a hot tech stock.

I tried above to make a case for my point of view.

Other analysts did likewise for their views.

As always, it’s up to you to decide for yourself which argument(s) you believe carry the day.

The price chart shows us how Mr. Market has been evaluating GLE.

Despite a recent and short-lived swoon, the 10-day exponential moving average (EMA), now rising, is doing so while remaining above the also-rising 50-day EMA.

Consider, too, Chaikin Money Flow (CMF) and the Chaikin Oscillator (CO). Both measure which party to trades is more motivated. CMF does it for institutional investors. CO does it for the market in general.

Both indicators have spent much of the past 12 months solidly ensconced in bullish territory. They slid recently, but now look to be climbing again.

The bears are finding it hard to sustain any edge when it comes to GLW. Apparently, many in the investment community, all of whom presumably know what GLW’s valuation ratios are, see its prospects similar to the way I do.

When it comes to rating stocks, I think in terms of probable future stock performance relative to the market, rather than literally buying, holding or selling.

And since I’m not rating based on a quant model, I’ll eschew “Strong …” extremes. Absent an exceptional degree of conviction, I think “Buy,” Hold,” or “Sell” are enough.

I see GLW as likely outperforming the broader market.

I translate that to the Seeking Alpha rating taxonomy by rating GLW as a “Buy.”

data.title: Zscaler: Short-Term Noise, Long-Term Opportunity
data.description:
Investment Thesis

After a more than 60% drop, Zscaler, Inc. (ZS) offers an attractive opportunity with the unique combination of strong growth, solid profit margins, and a rapidly expanding AI-enabled security platform. The company is still in solid shape financially despite the drop, with expected revenue growth of ~24% and an FCFM of ~21%. This has placed the company among the top software operators with a rule-of-62 profile and a rule-of-40 score above 50. This level of growth and profit is not common at Zscaler's size, and the combination of this expected growth and the current margin profile provides the foundation for a return to higher multiples.

Beyond financial performance, Zscaler has a competitive advantage through its placement within its customers' networks and systems. This advantage comes from its “Zero Trust Exchange,” which allows processing of nearly one trillion AI/ML transactions each year. As a result of this, the company has created a self-reinforcing cycle of improved threat detection and customer dependence on its solutions. Due to its historical database, which stretches over ten years, Zscaler has created a barrier to entry, making it harder for companies like Cloudflare, Inc. (NET) and Netskope, Inc. (NTSK) to replicate this technology at the same scale.

The company is also making changes to its growth model. A new revenue stream is being introduced as a result of the transition to usage-based pricing tied to AI workload consumption. Additionally, efforts such as the Zero Trust Everywhere (ZTE) initiative and the ZFlex program have the potential to increase average contract values, shorten sales cycles, and provide additional insight into long-term revenue potential. So far they have captured 40% of the Global 2000, with the majority of that in the Americas (57%), leaving room for further growth internationally in underpenetrated markets.

Even with these strong results, the stock is trading at levels that are significantly lower than both its historical levels and its peer group, with a forward revenue multiple of 5.7x and forward non-GAAP P/E of 29x. Given this valuation at Zscaler's high growth rates, I believe the market is too focused on the company's near-term AR issues and therefore failing to recognize the long-term viability and sustainability of its platform. For these reasons, I'm rating Zscaler a Buy.

Financials and the Rule-of-62

Q2 2026 was a strong quarter, with Zscaler’s revenue up 26% YoY at $815.8 million, beating its high-end guidance. Its $181 million non-GAAP income (22.2% margin) represents a 29% increase YoY, and its $1.01 per share non-GAAP EPS is $0.78 higher than the previous year, which showcases increasing operating leverage. In addition, Zscaler generated FCF of $169.1 million (21% margin), which brings TTM FCF to $947 million and gives the company a price/FCF multiple of less than 20x. Management also updated its FY 2026 guidance, upgrading on every front, with ARR expected to be up 24% YoY, non-GAAP income expected to be up 28-29% YoY, and EPS expected to grow by 22-23%. In addition, non-GAAP EPS is expected to be in the range of $3.99-4.02, equaling a ~22-23% growth rate. Overall, these results show a strong company that has continued to grow profitability at a fast rate; despite this, the stock's multiples have compressed to all-time lows.

With a SaaS profile like Zscaler's, the rule of 40 is a good metric to account for. However, it clears this easily with its 26% revenue growth and an H1 2026 FCFM of ~36%, bringing the score up to 62, which places it among the most elite firms.

The balance sheet doesn't seem to be a concern, with the company holding $3.51 billion in cash and a net position of $1.81 billion. Deferred revenue was $2.36 billion, or up 25% YoY, meaning Zscaler has continued to compound rather than burn cash. In my opinion, with only increased growth and a strong balance sheet, the fall in valuation has been overdone.

AI Security Moat

The Zscaler ThreatLabz 2026 AI Security Report makes a much stronger telemetry argument than any marketing pitch could. During calendar year 2025 alone, Zscaler processed nearly 1 trillion AI/ML transactions on behalf of about 9,000 enterprises, which is an ~83% YoY increase. Further, its systems have identified more than 3,400 distinct AI applications running within the customer environments, which has grown 4x YoY. Approximately 18,000 TB of data were also passed to AI applications via Zscaler’s inline inspection layer, displaying significant security concerns for unchecked systems.

This growth in transactions has enabled the company to develop a solid competitive advantage. Each time an enterprise passes an AI-related transaction through Zscaler’s ZTE, it improves the model used for threat detection. This creates a self-reinforcing cycle, where more customers means more data, and more data means improved detection. This all leads back to stronger detection and improved confidence in Zscaler's systems, which could draw demand. While peers such as Cloudflare and Netskope can compete with Zscaler on a per-seat basis in some areas of the marketplace, neither of these companies will be able to match its decade-long advantage in terms of telemetry related to enterprise traffic. This data moat is only set to improve, with new customers and applications improving the moat further.

The AI Protect platform, an integrated suite of AI asset management, AI access security, and AI red teaming, represents a governance framework for CISOs managing shadow AI, sanctioned AI, and agentic AI in one place. The acquisition of SquareX in February of this year provided Zscaler with the capability to extend ZTE to any browser for both unmanaged and BYOD devices, thereby filling a previously underserved area of security protection that VPNs and virtual desktop infrastructure have struggled to provide. By combining this acquisition with the existing capabilities of the Zscaler platform, IT decision makers are given a way to eliminate costly legacy products without sacrificing user experience.

Agentic AI is the next major development in AI technology, and Zscaler is positioned to lead early. Millions of MCP requests are already being processed by the company every month, and it was recently named as an early adoption partner for Microsoft Entra Agent ID to govern identities of non-human AI agents using similar methods as human users. This makes Zscaler an established early mover in a market that does not exist today. Additionally, non-seat-based metered usage currently accounts for greater than 25% of all new ACV, with ARR generated from this model doubling YoY. As enterprise AI workload scales, this usage-based pricing metric can grow independently of new customer logos, representing a material shift in how Zscaler grows. Through this transition, Zscaler can capture that higher-margin revenue, which will grow at a more exponential pace with the risk of AI agents, rather than acquiring new customers.

Platform Expansion and ZFlex Momentum

I believe that ZTE adoption is one of the most important metrics to look at currently. As of Q2, 550 Zscaler enterprise customers were using ZTE, which is 4x the 130 enterprise customers the company had last year. CEO Jay Chaudhry has also mentioned the ZTE importance, where when a customer moves their organization into ZTE, they see strong increases in ARR per account, with one customer's growing by 5x. Given that 550 enterprises have committed to using ZTE and the number of potential customers is increasing rapidly, they aren't required to continue adding customers to maintain growth.

The ZFlex Program is changing how Zscaler sells its services, and it also helps in understanding historical ARR metrics. Through Q2, ZFlex produced over $290 million in TCV, representing a 65% QoQ increase. On average, each of these contracts has a term length of approximately four years, making the sales cycle for each of the ZFlex contracts shorter, but initial commitments larger. Customers agree to purchase the entire platform at once and then activate or deactivate specific modules based upon their needs. For example, if a customer agrees to enter into a four-year contract under ZFlex, the ARR metric will capture only the first 12 months of contracted revenue associated with that agreement, not the full TCV. This makes RPO the best metric to track in this scenario, and with 31% growth to $6.1 billion, it shows the program's strength.

The customer cohort data demonstrates a strong trend of compounding, with 728 customers with $1 million+ in ARR, an 18% YoY increase. There are also 3,886 customers with $100K+ in ARR, also representing an 18% YoY increase. Lastly, ZDX Advanced Plus, which is an agent-based IT operations product, reached $100 million in LTM bookings, which shows higher growth of 80% YoY. ZDX was one of the company's newer products and is already generating $1 million+ run rate bookings.

In my view, the penetration gives me confidence in the company's sustainability of growth. While the current level of penetration is high, with Zscaler services at more than 45% of Fortune 500 companies, it leaves significant white space for growth opportunities within the mid-market and international markets. Specifically, European customers represent 28% of overall revenue, while Asia-Pacific-Japan customers represent only 15% of revenue. Lastly, the SASE market is expected to grow from $13.11 billion in 2026 to $77.08 billion by 2035, at a CAGR of 22.06%. This, combined with Zscaler's climbing market share, makes the growth of Zscaler more certain and, in my opinion, makes current multiples look cheap.

The Bear Case

A lot of concern was around guidance for full-year ARR, which increased by $35 million due to an upward revision of Red Canary's projected contribution but lacks organic ARR growth. Some see this as evidence of softening demand that is being disguised through acquisitions. A recent BTIG downgrade also sparked a downturn, citing concerns around competition. This surrounds companies like Cloudflare and Netskope, who offer similar services at lower prices, and firewall companies like Fortinet, Inc. (FTNT) and Palo Alto Networks, Inc. (PANW), who have done better jobs upselling SASE. In my view, the competitive threat is real; however, it is concentrated in the mid-market. Chaudhry stated during the Q2 earnings call that the company hasn't “seen much change in the competitive dynamics over the past few quarters” and that Q2 was marked by both a record number of conversions in the sales pipeline and record million-dollar-plus win deals.

Fortune 500 companies and Global 2000 companies also tend to place greater emphasis on things like compliance certification, the ability to integrate multiple platforms, and the deployment timing, rather than price. The company has achieved FedRAMP High Jab authorization and serves 14 of 15 U.S. cabinet-level departments, which represent switching cost moats. So while Cloudflare and Netskope are certainly competitors in the mid-market space, they are unlikely to disrupt Zscaler's strong enterprise customer base.

While there is some risk associated with the integration of Red Canary, it appears like a near-term headwind with minimal impact. CFO Kevin Rubin stated during the most recent call that MDR-related churn post-close has exceeded Zscaler's normal churn rates. On the other hand, while Red Canary may be experiencing elevated levels of churn, the company is still generating new MDR business at a rate that exceeds any lost legacy customers. This displays that the acquisition is working, even with some near-term integration risks.

Valuation

Zscaler's EV is approximately $17.33 billion, giving an EV/revenue multiple of ~5.8x and an EV/forward revenue multiple of ~5.2x. The forward non-GAAP P/E multiple is ~29x, and the forward price to FCF is ~19x, all of which are historically low for the company. Peer group comparison is also important. Palo Alto Networks, Inc. (PANW) currently trades at ~42x forward non-GAAP earnings, while CrowdStrike Holdings, Inc. (CRWD) currently trades at ~78x. Fortinet, Inc. (FTNT) is the least expensive of the larger peer group with a forward non-GAAP multiple of ~26x, making it the closest to Zscaler at ~28x. Zscaler's multiple is less expensive than each of its two largest enterprise peer companies on this basis, even though Zscaler reported significantly greater revenue growth acceleration in Q2 2026. This shows the market does not presently view Zscaler as being a category-leading company and believes it may have structural or competitive problems.

The multiples have swung widely over the past few months, with the stock trading at ~9.6x EV/revenue (using today's revenue estimates) in January 2026 at ~$217 per share. On an EV/forward revenue basis of ~5.2x, the valuation is now almost half what it was ten weeks prior without any meaningful change in fundamental trend. Given Zscaler's strong enterprise foundation and competitive advantage, I believe multiples will re-expand. In my view, a multiple of 7x EV/forward revenue is fair, if not conservative, given the company's growth profile. Applying this gives a price per share of ~$158.85, which is my 12-month price target and represents 35%+ upside.

Risks

Organic ARR decline is the most significant risk to the thesis. If upcoming earnings reveal further declines in organic net new ARR excluding Red Canary, the organic story will weaken. Although RPO and the TCV for ZFlex indicate an increase in forward commitment while the ARR may appear soft, the market may ultimately price what it can see in terms of ARR numbers and not the thesis surrounding why they are skewed.

The mid-market competitive pricing pressure against Zscaler remains one of the biggest concerns. If Zscaler cannot maintain its position within the mid-market and loses those customers to NET or Netskope, it could hurt longer-term market expansion plans.

Conclusion

Zscaler is a well-run enterprise software business whose long-term value proposition has been distorted by near-term uncertainty, not by fundamental changes in its long-term trajectory. The company's continued success demonstrates both strong operational execution across financial performance, platform reach, early adoption of AI-native security, and a unique ability to maintain an elite balance between growth and margin expansion.

Although there may be some concern regarding its organic ARR and competitive threats in the mid-market space, these factors have no material impact on either Zscaler's leading position in the enterprise or its long-term growth vectors. The company's deeply integrated presence within large enterprises, combined with regulatory certifications and switching costs, provides it with a highly durable competitive advantage, which is difficult to get rid of.

Currently, the market appears to be valuing Zscaler like a troubled growth company rather than a top-tier company with increasing margins and a widening moat due to its rapidly expanding use of AI-based technology. As execution continues and revenue visibility improves, with ARR updates and commitments related to ZFlex, I believe there exists substantial opportunity for Zscaler's multiples to re-expand. In my view, the risk/reward of Zscaler makes the company a compelling Buy, with my 12-month price target at $158.85.
